title: DORA: AI is an amplifier — returns come from platform quality, not the tools
summary: The DORA team's ROI of AI-Assisted Software Development report (2026.01), surfaced in InfoQ coverage in May, draws on ~5,000 technology professionals and 100+ hours of interviews to argue that AI investment payoff is gated by the underlying engineering system: internal platform quality, workflow clarity, and team alignment. The report introduces a J-Curve value-realization model and an interactive ROI calculator. Companion DORA findings show AI lifts throughput 2–18% but raises change-failure rates and technical debt where code-level observability is weak. For CTOs this reframes the AI rollout decision: organizations with fragmented processes will see AI magnify dysfunction, not paper over it.
whyItMatters: Throughput gains (2–18%) are real but modest — they will not justify large agent licensing on their own without platform investment.
The J-Curve framing gives leadership a defensible narrative for why year-1 returns are negative, useful in board-level ROI debates.
Stability regressions (failure rates, tech debt) shift the priority from 'adopt AI' to 'instrument the codebase first'.
recommendedActions: Before scaling AI coding tools, audit platform foundations: CI reliability, deployment frequency baseline, code-level observability.
Adopt the DORA ROI calculator as a default template in business-case approvals to discipline vendor claims.
Pair every productivity metric with a stability metric (CFR, MTTR, debt indicator) in exec dashboards.
risksAndCaveats: Survey-and-interview methodology — self-reported productivity is known to overstate AI gains.
DORA findings generalize across industries; regulated sectors should weight stability findings more heavily.
'Amplifier' framing is intuitive but hard to falsify; specific magnitude estimates carry wide confidence intervals.
